title: Building a timber cold frame for the garden
subtitle: Cut and assemble a sloped-lid timber box to protect seedlings without supplementary heating
excerpt: How to build a simple cold frame from treated softwood, with a sloped lid to shed rain and maximise light, using an old window frame or a sheet of polycarbonate as the glazing. Covers the box, the lid, and siting advice.
sourceNotes: RHS Growing Under Cover guidance (open access). Kitchen Garden Magazine. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: An unheated growing enclosure with a transparent or translucent lid, used to trap solar heat and protect plants from frost and wind without any added warmth. Typically used to harden off seedlings raised indoors before planting out, and to overwinter half-hardy perennials. The slope of the lid (facing south in the northern hemisphere) maximises the angle of incidence of winter sun and sheds rain away from the back wall.

content.text: is most useful when it is built around a glazing panel you already have, an old single-glazed window frame is ideal. This guide uses a window of approximately 900 × 600 mm; adjust the box dimensions to match your glazing. The box is made from 22 × 100 mm or 22 × 150 mm treated softwood boards, assembled with screwed butt joints. The entire project requires no specialist tools beyond those needed for basic carpentry.

Treated softwood boards: 22 × 150 mm, approximately 5 m total length (adjust for your glazing size). Two 75 mm butt hinges to attach the lid. 50 mm No. 10 exterior screws (stainless steel or galvanised). Exterior wood glue. One or two prop sticks (offcuts of timber dowel or batten) to hold the lid open for ventilation. If using polycarbonate rather than glass: 4 mm twin-wall polycarbonate sheet cut to size, with end caps to seal the flutes.

Cut the four wall boards to length. The front wall (facing south) is lower than the back wall; a front height of 200 mm and a back height of 300 mm gives a 100 mm drop over the depth of the frame, sufficient to shed rainwater without blocking winter sun. Cut the two side walls as a taper: mark 200 mm at the front corner and 300 mm at the back corner on each side board and cut the slope with the tenon saw. Check both side boards are identical by holding them face to face.

Lay the front board flat and position one side board at the end at 90 degrees. Apply exterior wood glue to the joint and drive two 50 mm screws through the side board into the end of the front board. Add the other side board, then the back board. Check the box is square by measuring diagonals. Allow the glue to cure before inverting to check all walls sit level on the ground.

Lay the glazed lid on top of the box and check the fit. The lid should overhang the front and sides by 10 to 20 mm to direct rainwater away from the joints. Mark the hinge positions on the back wall and the lid frame, 100 mm in from each end. Screw both hinges to the back wall first, then to the lid frame. Test the lid opens and closes fully without binding at the corners. Sand any tight spots with the sanding block.

Site the cold frame facing south on bare soil or on paving slabs. In spring, prop the lid open by 50 to 100 mm during the day to ventilate and prevent overheating; close it overnight when frost is forecast. Apply one coat of exterior wood preservative or oil to all external surfaces annually.

attrs.symptom: Box walls bowing outward after a season
attrs.cause: The boards are drying and cupping, or the screwed butt joints are loosening.
attrs.fix: Drive additional screws through the joints to pull them tight. Cut a timber cross-brace from an offcut and screw it diagonally inside the box to hold the walls square.

attrs.symptom: Condensation accumulates heavily inside and plants develop mould
attrs.cause: The lid is not being propped open enough during the day to exchange the humid air.
attrs.fix: Prop the lid higher during any mild day above about 5°C, even in winter. Good air movement prevents botrytis on overwintering plants.
